      97              : 
      98              : // On non-MSVC 64-bit targets, we expect __uint128_t support. This includes
      99              : // clang-cl, which defines both __clang__ and _MSC_VER.
     100              : #if (!defined(_MSC_VER) || defined(__clang__)) && defined(OPENSSL_64_BIT)
     101              : #define BORINGSSL_HAS_UINT128
     102              : typedef __int128_t int128_t;
     103              : typedef __uint128_t uint128_t;
     104              : 
     105              : // __uint128_t division depends on intrinsics in the compiler runtime. Those
     106              : // intrinsics are missing in clang-cl (https://crbug.com/787617) and nanolibc.
     107              : // These may be bugs in the toolchain definition, but just disable it for now.
     108              : // EDK2's toolchain is missing __udivti3 (b/339380897) so cannot support
     109              : // 128-bit division currently.
     110              : #if !defined(_MSC_VER) && !defined(OPENSSL_NANOLIBC) && \
     111              :     !defined(__EDK2_BORINGSSL__)
     112              : #define BORINGSSL_CAN_DIVIDE_UINT128
     113              : #endif
     114              : #endif
     115              : 
     116              : // GCC-like compilers indicate SSE2 with `__SSE2__`. MSVC leaves the caller to
     117              : // know that x86_64 has SSE2, and uses _M_IX86_FP to indicate SSE2 on x86.
     118              : // https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/preprocessor/predefined-macros?view=msvc-170
     119              : #if defined(__SSE2__) || defined(_M_AMD64) || defined(_M_X64) || \
     120              :     (defined(_M_IX86_FP) && _M_IX86_FP >= 2)
     121              : #define OPENSSL_SSE2
     122              : #endif
     123              : 
     124              : #if defined(OPENSSL_X86) && !defined(OPENSSL_NO_ASM) && !defined(OPENSSL_SSE2)
     125              : #error \
     126              :     "x86 assembly requires SSE2. Build with -msse2 (recommended), or disable assembly optimizations with -DOPENSSL_NO_ASM."
     127              : #endif
     128              : 
     129              : // For convenience in testing the fallback code, we allow disabling SSE2
     130              : // intrinsics via `OPENSSL_NO_SSE2_FOR_TESTING`. We require SSE2 on x86 and
     131              : // x86_64, so we would otherwise need to test such code on a non-x86 platform.
     132              : //
     133              : // This does not remove the above requirement for SSE2 support with assembly
     134              : // optimizations. It only disables some intrinsics-based optimizations so that
     135              : // we can test the fallback code on CI.
     136              : #if defined(OPENSSL_SSE2) && defined(OPENSSL_NO_SSE2_FOR_TESTING)
     137              : #undef OPENSSL_SSE2
     138              : #endif
     139              : 
     140              : #if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
     141              : #define OPENSSL_ATTR_CONST __attribute__((const))
     142              : #else
     143              : #define OPENSSL_ATTR_CONST
     144              : #endif

     151              : OPENSSL_EXPORT void OPENSSL_reset_malloc_counter_for_testing();

     153              : // OPENSSL_disable_malloc_failures_for_testing, when malloc testing is enabled,

     196              : // align_pointer returns `ptr`, advanced to `alignment`. `alignment` must be a

     203            0 :   // offset and advance in pointer space. C guarantees that casting from pointer

     213              : 
     214              : // Constant-time utility functions.
     215              : //
     216              : // The following methods return a bitmask of all ones (0xff...f) for true and 0
     217              : // for false. This is useful for choosing a value based on the result of a
     218              : // conditional in constant time. For example,
     219              : //
     220              : // if (a < b) {
     221              : //   c = a;
     222              : // } else {
     223              : //   c = b;
     224              : // }
     225              : //
     226              : // can be written as
     227              : //
     228              : // crypto_word_t lt = constant_time_lt_w(a, b);
     229              : // c = constant_time_select_w(lt, a, b);
     230              : 
     231              : // crypto_word_t is the type that most constant-time functions use. Ideally we
     232              : // would like it to be `size_t`, but NaCl builds in 64-bit mode with 32-bit
     233              : // pointers, which means that `size_t` can be 32 bits when `BN_ULONG` is 64
     234              : // bits. Since we want to be able to do constant-time operations on a
     235              : // `BN_ULONG`, `crypto_word_t` is defined as an unsigned value with the native
     236              : // word length.
     237              : #if defined(OPENSSL_64_BIT)
     238              : typedef uint64_t crypto_word_t;
     239              : #elif defined(OPENSSL_32_BIT)
     240              : typedef uint32_t crypto_word_t;
     241              : #else
     242              : #error "Must define either OPENSSL_32_BIT or OPENSSL_64_BIT"
     243              : #endif
     244              : 
     245              : #define CONSTTIME_TRUE_W ~((crypto_word_t)0)
     246              : #define CONSTTIME_FALSE_W ((crypto_word_t)0)
     247              : #define CONSTTIME_TRUE_8 ((uint8_t)0xff)
     248              : #define CONSTTIME_FALSE_8 ((uint8_t)0)
     249              : 
     250              : // value_barrier_w returns `a`, but prevents GCC and Clang from reasoning about
     251              : // the returned value. This is used to mitigate compilers undoing constant-time
     252              : // code, until we can express our requirements directly in the language.
     253              : //
     254              : // Note the compiler is aware that `value_barrier_w` has no side effects and
     255              : // always has the same output for a given input. This allows it to eliminate
     256              : // dead code, move computations across loops, and vectorize.
     257            0 : inline crypto_word_t value_barrier_w(crypto_word_t a) {
     258            0 : #if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
     259            0 :   __asm__("" : "+r"(a) : /* no inputs */);
     260            0 : #endif
     261            0 :   return a;
     262            0 : }
     263              : 
     264              : // value_barrier_u32 behaves like `value_barrier_w` but takes a `uint32_t`.
     265            0 : inline uint32_t value_barrier_u32(uint32_t a) {
     266            0 : #if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
     267            0 :   __asm__("" : "+r"(a) : /* no inputs */);
     268            0 : #endif
     269            0 :   return a;
     270            0 : }
     271              : 
     272              : // value_barrier_u64 behaves like `value_barrier_w` but takes a `uint64_t`.
     273            0 : inline uint64_t value_barrier_u64(uint64_t a) {
     274            0 : #if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
     275            0 :   __asm__("" : "+r"(a) : /* no inputs */);
     276            0 : #endif
     277            0 :   return a;
     278            0 : }
     279              : 
     280              : // `value_barrier_u8` could be defined as above, but compilers other than
     281              : // clang seem to still materialize 0x00..00MM instead of reusing 0x??..??MM.
     282              : 
     283              : // constant_time_msb_w returns the given value with the MSB copied to all the
     284              : // other bits.
     285            0 : inline crypto_word_t constant_time_msb_w(crypto_word_t a) {
     286            0 :   return 0u - (a >> (sizeof(a) * 8 - 1));
     287            0 : }
     288              : 
     289              : // constant_time_lt_w returns 0xff..f if a < b and 0 otherwise.
     290            0 : inline crypto_word_t constant_time_lt_w(crypto_word_t a, crypto_word_t b) {
     291            0 :   // Consider the two cases of the problem:
     292            0 :   //   msb(a) == msb(b): a < b iff the MSB of a - b is set.
     293            0 :   //   msb(a) != msb(b): a < b iff the MSB of b is set.
     294            0 :   //
     295            0 :   // If msb(a) == msb(b) then the following evaluates as:
     296            0 :   //   msb(a^((a^b)|((a-b)^a))) ==
     297            0 :   //   msb(a^((a-b) ^ a))       ==   (because msb(a^b) == 0)
     298            0 :   //   msb(a^a^(a-b))           ==   (rearranging)
     299            0 :   //   msb(a-b)                      (because ∀x. x^x == 0)
     300            0 :   //
     301            0 :   // Else, if msb(a) != msb(b) then the following evaluates as:
     302            0 :   //   msb(a^((a^b)|((a-b)^a))) ==
     303            0 :   //   msb(a^(𝟙 | ((a-b)^a)))   ==   (because msb(a^b) == 1 and 𝟙
     304            0 :   //                                  represents a value s.t. msb(𝟙) = 1)
     305            0 :   //   msb(a^𝟙)                 ==   (because ORing with 1 results in 1)
     306            0 :   //   msb(b)
     307            0 :   //
     308            0 :   //
     309            0 :   // Here is an SMT-LIB verification of this formula:
     310            0 :   //
     311            0 :   // (define-fun lt ((a (_ BitVec 32)) (b (_ BitVec 32))) (_ BitVec 32)
     312            0 :   //   (bvxor a (bvor (bvxor a b) (bvxor (bvsub a b) a)))
     313            0 :   // )
     314            0 :   //
     315            0 :   // (declare-fun a () (_ BitVec 32))
     316            0 :   // (declare-fun b () (_ BitVec 32))
     317            0 :   //
     318            0 :   // (assert (not (= (= #x00000001 (bvlshr (lt a b) #x0000001f)) (bvult a b))))
     319            0 :   // (check-sat)
     320            0 :   // (get-model)
     321            0 :   return constant_time_msb_w(a ^ ((a ^ b) | ((a - b) ^ a)));
     322            0 : }

     385              : // constant_time_select_w returns (mask & a) | (~mask & b). When `mask` is all

     388              : inline crypto_word_t constant_time_select_w(crypto_word_t mask, crypto_word_t a,
     389            0 :                                             crypto_word_t b) {

     391            0 :   // to a cmov, it sometimes further transforms it into a branch, which we do
     392            0 :   // not want.
     393            0 :   //
     394            0 :   // Hiding the value of the mask from the compiler evades this transformation.
     395            0 :   mask = value_barrier_w(mask);
     396            0 :   return (mask & a) | (~mask & b);

     405            0 :   // to materialize 0x????..?MM instead, but only clang is that clever.
     406            0 :   // However, vectorization of bitwise operations seems to work better on

     408            0 :   // `uint8_t` after the value barrier but before the bitwise operations.
     409            0 :   uint8_t m = value_barrier_w(mask);

     428              : // constant_time_conditional_memcpy copies `n` bytes from `src` to `dst` if

     440            0 : }
     441              : 
     442              : // constant_time_conditional_memxor xors `n` bytes from `src` to `dst` if
     443              : // `mask` is 0xff..ff and does nothing if `mask` is 0. The `n`-byte memory
     444              : // ranges at `dst` and `src` must not overlap, as when calling `memcpy`.
     445              : inline void constant_time_conditional_memxor(void *dst, const void *src,
     446              :                                              size_t n,
     447            0 :                                              const crypto_word_t mask) {
     448            0 :   assert(!buffers_alias(dst, n, src, n));
     449            0 :   uint8_t *out = (uint8_t *)dst;
     450            0 :   const uint8_t *in = (const uint8_t *)src;
     451            0 : #if defined(__GNUC__) || defined(__clang__)
     452              :   // gcc 13.2.0 doesn't automatically vectorize this loop regardless of barrier
     453              :   // clang 16.0.6 vectorizes if alias analysis finds src and dst are disjoint,
     454              :   // but does not conclude that from the !buffers_alias assert.
     455            0 :   typedef uint8_t v32u8 __attribute__((vector_size(32), aligned(1), may_alias));
     456            0 :   size_t n_vec = n & ~(size_t)31;
     457            0 :   v32u8 masks = ((uint8_t)mask - (v32u8){});  // broadcast
     458            0 :   for (size_t i = 0; i < n_vec; i += 32) {
     459            0 :     *(v32u8 *)&out[i] ^= masks & *(v32u8 *)&in[i];
     460            0 :   }
     461            0 :   in += n_vec;
     462            0 :   out += n_vec;
     463            0 :   n -= n_vec;
     464            0 : #endif
     465            0 :   for (size_t i = 0; i < n; i++) {
     466            0 :     out[i] ^= value_barrier_w(mask) & in[i];
     467            0 :   }
     468            0 : }
     469              : 
     470              : #if defined(BORINGSSL_CONSTANT_TIME_VALIDATION)
     471              : 
     472              : // CONSTTIME_SECRET takes a pointer and a number of bytes and marks that region
     473              : // of memory as secret. Secret data is tracked as it flows to registers and
     474              : // other parts of a memory. If secret data is used as a condition for a branch,
     475              : // or as a memory index, it will trigger warnings in valgrind.
     476              : #define CONSTTIME_SECRET(ptr, len) VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_UNDEFINED(ptr, len)
     477              : 
     478              : // CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY takes a pointer and a number of bytes and marks that
     479              : // region of memory as public. Public data is not subject to constant-time
     480              : // rules.
     481              : #define CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(ptr, len) VALGRIND_MAKE_MEM_DEFINED(ptr, len)
     482              : 
     483              : #else
     484              : 
     485              : // Just disable unused warnings for those.
     486              : #define CONSTTIME_SECRET(ptr, len) \
     487              :   do {                             \
     488              :     (void)(ptr);                   \
     489              :     (void)(len);                   \
     490              :   } while (false)
     491              : #define CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(ptr, len) \
     492            0 :   do {                                 \
     493            0 :     (void)(ptr);                       \
     494            0 :     (void)(len);                       \
     495            0 :   } while (false)
     496              : 
     497              : #endif  // BORINGSSL_CONSTANT_TIME_VALIDATION
     498              : 
     499            0 : inline crypto_word_t constant_time_declassify_w(crypto_word_t v) {
     500            0 :   // Return `v` through a value barrier to be safe. Valgrind-based constant-time
     501            0 :   // validation is partly to check the compiler has not undone any constant-time
     502            0 :   // work. Any place `BORINGSSL_CONSTANT_TIME_VALIDATION` influences
     503            0 :   // optimizations, this validation is inaccurate.
     504            0 :   //
     505            0 :   // However, by sending pointers through valgrind, we likely inhibit escape
     506            0 :   // analysis. On local variables, particularly booleans, we likely
     507            0 :   // significantly impact optimizations.
     508            0 :   //
     509            0 :   // Thus, to be safe, stick a value barrier, in hopes of comparably inhibiting
     510            0 :   // compiler analysis.
     511            0 :   CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(&v, sizeof(v));
     512            0 :   return value_barrier_w(v);
     513            0 : }
     514              : 
     515            0 : inline int constant_time_declassify_int(int v) {
     516            0 :   static_assert(sizeof(uint32_t) == sizeof(int),
     517            0 :                 "int is not the same size as uint32_t");
     518              :   // See comment above.
     519            0 :   CONSTTIME_DECLASSIFY(&v, sizeof(v));
     520            0 :   return value_barrier_u32(v);
     521            0 : }
     522              : 
     523              : // declassify_assert behaves like `assert` but declassifies the result of
     524              : // evaluating `expr`. This allows the assertion to branch on the (presumably
     525              : // public) result, but still ensures that values leading up to the computation
     526              : // were secret.
     527            0 : #define declassify_assert(expr) assert(constant_time_declassify_int(expr))

     529              : 
     530              : // Thread-safe initialisation.
     531              : 
     532              : #if !defined(OPENSSL_THREADS)
     533              : typedef uint32_t CRYPTO_once_t;
     534              : #define CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT 0
     535              : #elif defined(OPENSSL_WINDOWS_THREADS)
     536              : typedef INIT_ONCE CRYPTO_once_t;
     537              : #define CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT INIT_ONCE_STATIC_INIT
     538              : #elif defined(OPENSSL_PTHREADS)
     539              : typedef pthread_once_t CRYPTO_once_t;
     540              : #define CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT PTHREAD_ONCE_INIT
     541              : #else
     542              : #error "Unknown threading library"
     543              : #endif
     544              : 
     545              : // CRYPTO_once calls `init` exactly once per process. This is thread-safe: if
     546              : // concurrent threads call `CRYPTO_once` with the same `CRYPTO_once_t` argument
     547              : // then they will block until `init` completes, but `init` will have only been
     548              : // called once.
     549              : //
     550              : // The `once` argument must be a `CRYPTO_once_t` that has been initialised with
     551              : // the value `CRYPTO_ONCE_INIT`.
     552              : OPENSSL_EXPORT void CRYPTO_once(CRYPTO_once_t *once, void (*init)());
     553              : 
     554              : 
     555              : // Atomics.
     556              : //
     557              : // This is a thin wrapper over std::atomic because some embedded platforms do
     558              : // not support threads and don't provide a trivial std::atomic implementation.
     559              : // For now, this does not wrap std::memory_order. If we ever use non-default
     560              : // std::memory_order, we will need to wrap these too, or fix the embedded
     561              : // platforms to provide a no-op std::atomic. See https://crbug.com/442112336.
     562              : 
     563              : #if defined(OPENSSL_THREADS)
     564              : template <typename T>
     565              : using Atomic = std::atomic<T>;
     566              : #else
     567              : template <typename T>
     568              : class Atomic {
     569              :  public:
     570              :   static_assert(std::is_integral_v<T> || std::is_pointer_v<T>);
     571              : 
     572              :   Atomic() = default;
     573              :   constexpr Atomic(T value) : value_(value) {}
     574              :   Atomic(const Atomic &) = delete;
     575              :   Atomic &operator=(const Atomic &) = delete;
     576              :   T operator=(T value) {
     577              :     value_ = value;
     578              :     return value_;
     579              :   }
     580              : 
     581              :   T load() const { return value_; }
     582              :   void store(T desired) { value_ = desired; }
     583              : 
     584              :   bool compare_exchange_strong(T &expected, T desired) {
     585              :     if (value_ != expected) {
     586              :       expected = value_;
     587              :       return false;
     588              :     }
     589              :     value_ = desired;
     590              :     return true;
     591              :   }
     592              :   bool compare_exchange_weak(T &expected, T desired) {
     593              :     return compare_exchange_strong(expected, desired);
     594              :   }
     595              : 
     596              :   T exchange(T desired) { return std::exchange(value_, desired); }
     597              : 
     598              :  private:
     599              :   T value_;
     600              : };
     601              : #endif
     602              : 
     603              : 
     604              : // Reference counting.
     605              : 
     606              : // CRYPTO_REFCOUNT_MAX is the value at which the reference count saturates.
     607              : #define CRYPTO_REFCOUNT_MAX 0xffffffff
     608              : 
     609              : using CRYPTO_refcount_t = Atomic<uint32_t>;
     610              : 
     611              : // CRYPTO_refcount_inc atomically increments the value at `*count` unless the
     612              : // value would overflow. It's safe for multiple threads to concurrently call
     613              : // this or `CRYPTO_refcount_dec_and_test_zero` on the same
     614              : // `CRYPTO_refcount_t`.
     615              : OPENSSL_EXPORT void CRYPTO_refcount_inc(CRYPTO_refcount_t *count);
     616              : 
     617              : // CRYPTO_refcount_dec_and_test_zero tests the value at `*count`:
     618              : //   if it's zero, it crashes the address space.
     619              : //   if it's the maximum value, it returns zero.
     620              : //   otherwise, it atomically decrements it and returns one iff the resulting
     621              : //       value is zero.
     622              : //
     623              : // It's safe for multiple threads to concurrently call this or
     624              : // `CRYPTO_refcount_inc` on the same `CRYPTO_refcount_t`.
     625              : OPENSSL_EXPORT int CRYPTO_refcount_dec_and_test_zero(CRYPTO_refcount_t *count);
     626              : 
     627              : 
     628              : // Locks.
     629              : 
     630              : // A Mutex is a read/write lock. It can be constant-initialized, but has a
     631              : // destructor. To allocate a global one, use StaticMutex, which skips the
     632              : // destructor.
     633              : class OPENSSL_EXPORT StaticMutex {
     634              :  public:
     635              :   constexpr StaticMutex() = default;
     636              :   StaticMutex(const StaticMutex &) = delete;
     637              :   StaticMutex &operator=(const StaticMutex &) = delete;
     638              : 
     639              :   // LockRead locks the mutex such that other threads may also have a read lock,
     640              :   // but none may have a write lock.
     641              :   void LockRead();
     642              :   // UnlockRead releases a read lock.
     643              :   void UnlockRead();
     644              : 
     645              :   // LockWrite locks the mutex such that no other thread has any type of lock on
     646              :   // it.
     647              :   void LockWrite();
     648              :   // UnlockWrite releases a write lock.
     649              :   void UnlockWrite();
     650              : 
     651              : protected:
     652              : #if !defined(OPENSSL_THREADS)
     653              :   // Nothing.
     654              : #elif defined(OPENSSL_WINDOWS_THREADS)
     655              :   SRWLOCK lock_ = SRWLOCK_INIT;
     656              : #elif defined(OPENSSL_PTHREADS)
     657              :   pthread_rwlock_t lock_ = PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER;
     658              : #else
     659              : #error "Unknown threading library"
     660              : #endif
     661              : };
     662              : 
     663              : class OPENSSL_EXPORT Mutex  : public StaticMutex {
     664              :  public:
     665              :   constexpr Mutex() = default;
     666              :   ~Mutex();
     667              : };
     668              : 
     669              : namespace internal {
     670              : 
     671              : // MutexLockBase is a RAII helper for Mutex locking.
     672              : template <void (StaticMutex::*LockMethod)(),
     673              :           void (StaticMutex::*ReleaseMethod)()>
     674              : class MutexLockBase {
     675              :  public:
     676              :   explicit MutexLockBase(StaticMutex *mu) : mu_(mu) {
     677              :     assert(mu_ != nullptr);
     678              :     (mu_->*LockMethod)();
     679              :   }
     680              :   ~MutexLockBase() { (mu_->*ReleaseMethod)(); }
     681              :   MutexLockBase(const MutexLockBase &) = delete;
     682              :   MutexLockBase &operator=(const MutexLockBase &) = delete;
     683              : 
     684              :  private:
     685              :   StaticMutex *const mu_;
     686              : };
     687              : 
     688              : }  // namespace internal
     689              : 
     690              : using MutexWriteLock =
     691              :     internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::LockWrite, &StaticMutex::UnlockWrite>;
     692              : using MutexReadLock =
     693              :     internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::LockRead, &StaticMutex::UnlockRead>;
     694              : using MutexWriteUnlock =
     695              :     internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::UnlockWrite, &StaticMutex::LockWrite>;
     696              : using MutexReadUnlock =
     697              :     internal::MutexLockBase<&StaticMutex::UnlockRead, &StaticMutex::LockRead>;
     698              : 
     699              : 
     700              : // Thread local storage.
     701              : 
     702              : // thread_local_data_t enumerates the types of thread-local data that can be
     703              : // stored.
     704              : typedef enum {
     705              :   OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_ERR = 0,
     706              :   OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_RAND,
     707              :   OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_FIPS_COUNTERS,
     708              :   OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_FIPS_SERVICE_INDICATOR_STATE,
     709              :   OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCAL_TEST,
     710              :   NUM_OPENSSL_THREAD_LOCALS,
     711              : } thread_local_data_t;
     712              : 
     713              : // thread_local_destructor_t is the type of a destructor function that will be
     714              : // called when a thread exits and its thread-local storage needs to be freed.
     715              : typedef void (*thread_local_destructor_t)(void *);
     716              : 
     717              : // CRYPTO_get_thread_local gets the pointer value that is stored for the
     718              : // current thread for the given index, or NULL if none has been set.
     719              : OPENSSL_EXPORT void *CRYPTO_get_thread_local(thread_local_data_t value);
     720              : 
     721              : // CRYPTO_set_thread_local sets a pointer value for the current thread at the
     722              : // given index. This function should only be called once per thread for a given
     723              : // `index`: rather than update the pointer value itself, update the data that
     724              : // is pointed to.
     725              : //
     726              : // The destructor function will be called when a thread exits to free this
     727              : // thread-local data. All calls to `CRYPTO_set_thread_local` with the same
     728              : // `index` should have the same `destructor` argument. The destructor may be
     729              : // called with a NULL argument if a thread that never set a thread-local
     730              : // pointer for `index`, exits. The destructor may be called concurrently with
     731              : // different arguments.
     732              : //
     733              : // This function returns one on success or zero on error. If it returns zero
     734              : // then `destructor` has been called with `value` already.
     735              : OPENSSL_EXPORT int CRYPTO_set_thread_local(
     736              :     thread_local_data_t index, void *value,
     737              :     thread_local_destructor_t destructor);

     750              : struct ExDataFuncs;
     751              : 
     752              : // ExDataClass tracks the ex_indices registered for a type which
     753              : // supports ex_data. It should defined as a static global within the module
     754              : // which defines that type.
     755              : struct ExDataClass {
     756              :   explicit constexpr ExDataClass(bool with_app_data = false)
     757            0 :       : num_reserved(with_app_data ? 1 : 0) {}
     758              : 
     759              :   StaticMutex lock;
     760              :   // funcs is a linked list of `ExDataFuncs` structures. It may be traversed
     761              :   // without serialization only up to `num_funcs`. last points to the final
     762              :   // entry of `funcs`, or nullptr if empty.
     763              :   ExDataFuncs *funcs = nullptr, *last = nullptr;

     765              :   Atomic<uint32_t> num_funcs = 0;

     767              :   // `TYPE_get_app_data` functions.
     768              :   uint8_t num_reserved = 0;
     769              : };

     795              : // Endianness conversions.
     796              : 
     797              : #if defined(__GNUC__) && __GNUC__ >= 2
     798            0 : inline uint16_t CRYPTO_bswap2(uint16_t x) { return __builtin_bswap16(x); }
     799              : 
     800      4174280 : inline uint32_t CRYPTO_bswap4(uint32_t x) { return __builtin_bswap32(x); }
     801              : 
     802            0 : inline uint64_t CRYPTO_bswap8(uint64_t x) { return __builtin_bswap64(x); }
     803              : #elif defined(_MSC_VER)
     804              : #pragma intrinsic(_byteswap_uint64, _byteswap_ulong, _byteswap_ushort)
     805              : inline uint16_t CRYPTO_bswap2(uint16_t x) { return _byteswap_ushort(x); }
     806              : 
     807              : inline uint32_t CRYPTO_bswap4(uint32_t x) { return _byteswap_ulong(x); }
     808              : 
     809              : inline uint64_t CRYPTO_bswap8(uint64_t x) { return _byteswap_uint64(x); }
     810              : #else
     811              : inline uint16_t CRYPTO_bswap2(uint16_t x) { return (x >> 8) | (x << 8); }
     812              : 
     813              : inline uint32_t CRYPTO_bswap4(uint32_t x) {
     814              :   x = (x >> 16) | (x << 16);
     815              :   x = ((x & 0xff00ff00) >> 8) | ((x & 0x00ff00ff) << 8);
     816              :   return x;
     817              : }
     818              : 
     819              : inline uint64_t CRYPTO_bswap8(uint64_t x) {
     820              :   return CRYPTO_bswap4(x >> 32) | (((uint64_t)CRYPTO_bswap4(x)) << 32);
     821              : }
     822              : #endif
     823              : 
     824              : 
     825              : // Language bug workarounds.
     826              : //
     827              : // Most C standard library functions are undefined if passed NULL, even when the
     828              : // corresponding length is zero. This gives them (and, in turn, all functions
     829              : // which call them) surprising behavior on empty arrays. Some compilers will
     830              : // miscompile code due to this rule. See also
     831              : // https://www.imperialviolet.org/2016/06/26/nonnull.html
     832              : //
     833              : // These wrapper functions behave the same as the corresponding C standard
     834              : // functions, but behave as expected when passed NULL if the length is zero.
     835              : //
     836              : // Note `OPENSSL_memcmp` is a different function from `CRYPTO_memcmp`.
     837              : 
     838              : // C++ defines `memchr` as a const-correct overload.
     839            0 : inline const void *OPENSSL_memchr(const void *s, int c, size_t n) {
     840            0 :   if (n == 0) {
     841            0 :     return nullptr;
     842            0 :   }
     843            0 : 
     844            0 :   return memchr(s, c, n);
     845            0 : }
     846              : 
     847            0 : inline void *OPENSSL_memchr(void *s, int c, size_t n) {
     848            0 :   if (n == 0) {
     849            0 :     return nullptr;
     850            0 :   }
     851            0 : 
     852            0 :   return memchr(s, c, n);
     853            0 : }
     854              : 
     855            0 : inline int OPENSSL_memcmp(const void *s1, const void *s2, size_t n) {
     856            0 :   if (n == 0) {
     857            0 :     return 0;
     858            0 :   }
     859            0 : 
     860            0 :   return memcmp(s1, s2, n);
     861            0 : }
     862              : 
     863      4334851 : inline void *OPENSSL_memcpy(void *dst, const void *src, size_t n) {
     864      4334851 :   if (n == 0) {
     865            0 :     return dst;
     866            0 :   }
     867              : 
     868      4334851 :   return memcpy(dst, src, n);
     869      4334851 : }
     870              : 
     871            0 : inline void *OPENSSL_memmove(void *dst, const void *src, size_t n) {
     872            0 :   if (n == 0) {
     873            0 :     return dst;
     874            0 :   }
     875              : 
     876            0 :   return memmove(dst, src, n);
     877            0 : }
     878              : 
     879       481673 : inline void *OPENSSL_memset(void *dst, int c, size_t n) {
     880       481673 :   if (n == 0) {
     881            0 :     return dst;
     882            0 :   }
     883              : 
     884       481673 :   return memset(dst, c, n);
     885       481673 : }

     888              : // Loads and stores.
     889              : //
     890              : // The following functions load and store sized integers with the specified
     891              : // endianness. They use `memcpy`, and so avoid alignment or strict aliasing
     892              : // requirements on the input and output pointers.
     893              : 
     894            0 : inline uint16_t CRYPTO_load_u16_le(const void *in) {
     895            0 :   uint16_t v;
     896            0 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(&v, in, sizeof(v));
     897            0 :   return v;
     898            0 : }
     899              : 
     900            0 : inline void CRYPTO_store_u16_le(void *out, uint16_t v) {
     901            0 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(out, &v, sizeof(v));
     902            0 : }
     903              : 
     904            0 : inline uint16_t CRYPTO_load_u16_be(const void *in) {
     905            0 :   uint16_t v;
     906            0 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(&v, in, sizeof(v));
     907            0 :   return CRYPTO_bswap2(v);
     908            0 : }
     909              : 
     910            0 : inline void CRYPTO_store_u16_be(void *out, uint16_t v) {
     911            0 :   v = CRYPTO_bswap2(v);
     912            0 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(out, &v, sizeof(v));
     913            0 : }
     914              : 
     915            0 : inline uint32_t CRYPTO_load_u32_le(const void *in) {
     916            0 :   uint32_t v;
     917            0 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(&v, in, sizeof(v));
     918            0 :   return v;
     919            0 : }
     920              : 
     921            0 : inline void CRYPTO_store_u32_le(void *out, uint32_t v) {
     922            0 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(out, &v, sizeof(v));
     923            0 : }
     924              : 
     925      2568925 : inline uint32_t CRYPTO_load_u32_be(const void *in) {
     926      2568925 :   uint32_t v;
     927      2568925 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(&v, in, sizeof(v));
     928      2568925 :   return CRYPTO_bswap4(v);
     929      2568925 : }
     930              : 
     931      1605570 : inline void CRYPTO_store_u32_be(void *out, uint32_t v) {
     932      1605570 :   v = CRYPTO_bswap4(v);
     933      1605570 :   OPENSSL_memcpy(out, &v, sizeof(v));

     980              : // Bit rotation functions.

     992            0 : }

    1031              : int boringssl_self_test_startup();
    1032              : 
    1033              : // boringssl_ensure_rsa_sign_self_test checks whether the RSA signing self-test

    1035              : // address space if unsuccessful.

    1037              : 
    1038              : // boringssl_ensure_rsa_verify_self_test checks whether the RSA verification
    1039              : // self-test has been run in this address space. If not, it runs it and crashes

    1047              :

    1051              : void boringssl_ensure_ffdh_self_test();
    1052              : 
    1053              : #else
    1054              : 
    1055              : // Outside of FIPS mode, the lazy tests are no-ops.
    1056              : 
    1057            0 : inline void boringssl_ensure_rsa_sign_self_test() {}
    1058            0 : inline void boringssl_ensure_rsa_verify_self_test() {}
    1059            0 : inline void boringssl_ensure_ecc_self_test() {}
    1060            0 : inline void boringssl_ensure_ffdh_self_test() {}
    1061              : 
    1062              : #endif  // FIPS

    1103              : 
    1104              : // Runtime CPU feature support
    1105              : 
    1106              : #if defined(OPENSSL_X86) || defined(OPENSSL_X86_64)
    1107              : // OPENSSL_ia32cap_P contains the Intel CPUID bits when running on an x86 or
    1108              : // x86-64 system.
    1109              : //
    1110              : //   Index 0:
    1111              : //     EDX for CPUID where EAX = 1
    1112              : //     Bit 30 is used to indicate an Intel CPU
    1113              : //   Index 1:
    1114              : //     ECX for CPUID where EAX = 1
    1115              : //   Index 2:
    1116              : //     EBX for CPUID where EAX = 7, ECX = 0
    1117              : //     Bit 14 (for removed feature MPX) is used to indicate a preference for ymm
    1118              : //       registers over zmm even when zmm registers are supported
    1119              : //   Index 3:
    1120              : //     ECX for CPUID where EAX = 7, ECX = 0
    1121              : //
    1122              : // Note: the CPUID bits are pre-adjusted for the OSXSAVE bit and the XMM, YMM,
    1123              : // and AVX512 bits in XCR0, so it is not necessary to check those. (WARNING: See
    1124              : // caveats in cpu_intel.c.)
    1125              : //
    1126              : // This symbol should only be accessed with `OPENSSL_get_ia32cap`.
    1127              : extern uint32_t OPENSSL_ia32cap_P[4];
    1128              : 
    1129              : // OPENSSL_get_ia32cap initializes the library if needed and returns the `idx`th
    1130              : // entry of `OPENSSL_ia32cap_P`. It is marked as a const function so duplicate
    1131              : // calls can be merged by the compiler, at least when indices match.
    1132              : OPENSSL_ATTR_CONST uint32_t OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(int idx);
    1133              : 
    1134              : // OPENSSL_adjust_ia32cap adjusts `cap`, which should contain
    1135              : // `OPENSSL_ia32cap_P`, based on the environment variable value in `env`. This
    1136              : // function is exposed for unit tests.
    1137              : void OPENSSL_adjust_ia32cap(uint32_t cap[4], const char *env);
    1138              : 
    1139              : // See Intel manual, volume 2A, table 3-11.
    1140              : 
    1141            0 : inline int CRYPTO_is_intel_cpu() {
    1142            0 :   // The reserved bit 30 is used to indicate an Intel CPU.
    1143            0 :   return (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(0) & (1u << 30)) != 0;
    1144            0 : }
    1145              : 
    1146              : // See Intel manual, volume 2A, table 3-10.
    1147              :

    1249              : // CRYPTO_cpu_perf_is_like_silvermont returns one if, based on a heuristic, the
    1250              : // CPU has Silvermont-like performance characteristics. It is often faster to
    1251              : // run different codepaths on these CPUs than the available instructions would
    1252              : // otherwise select. See chacha-x86_64.pl.
    1253              : //
    1254              : // Bonnell, Silvermont's predecessor in the Atom lineup, will also be matched by
    1255              : // this. Goldmont (Silvermont's successor in the Atom lineup) added XSAVE so it
    1256              : // isn't matched by this. Various sources indicate AMD first implemented MOVBE
    1257              : // and XSAVE at the same time in Jaguar, so it seems like AMD chips will not be
    1258              : // matched by this. That seems to be the case for other x86(-64) CPUs.
    1259            0 : inline int CRYPTO_cpu_perf_is_like_silvermont() {
    1260            0 :   // WARNING: This MUST NOT be used to guard the execution of the XSAVE
    1261            0 :   // instruction. This is the "hardware supports XSAVE" bit, not the OSXSAVE bit
    1262            0 :   // that indicates whether we can safely execute XSAVE. This bit may be set
    1263            0 :   // even when XSAVE is disabled (by the operating system). See how the users of
    1264            0 :   // this bit use it.
    1265            0 :   //
    1266            0 :   // Historically, the XSAVE bit was artificially cleared on Knights Landing
    1267            0 :   // and Knights Mill chips, but as Intel has removed all support from GCC,
    1268            0 :   // LLVM, and SDE, we assume they are no longer worth special-casing.
    1269            0 :   int hardware_supports_xsave = (OPENSSL_get_ia32cap(1) & (1u << 26)) != 0;
    1270            0 :   return !hardware_supports_xsave && CRYPTO_is_MOVBE_capable();
    1271            0 : }

    1314              : 
    1315              : #if defined(OPENSSL_ARM) || defined(OPENSSL_AARCH64)
    1316              : 
    1317              : // ARMV7_NEON indicates support for NEON.
    1318              : #define ARMV7_NEON (1 << 0)
    1319              : 
    1320              : // ARMV8_AES indicates support for hardware AES instructions.
    1321              : #define ARMV8_AES (1 << 2)
    1322              : 
    1323              : // ARMV8_SHA1 indicates support for hardware SHA-1 instructions.
    1324              : #define ARMV8_SHA1 (1 << 3)
    1325              : 
    1326              : // ARMV8_SHA256 indicates support for hardware SHA-256 instructions.
    1327              : #define ARMV8_SHA256 (1 << 4)
    1328              : 
    1329              : // ARMV8_PMULL indicates support for carryless multiplication.
    1330              : #define ARMV8_PMULL (1 << 5)
    1331              : 
    1332              : // ARMV8_SHA512 indicates support for hardware SHA-512 instructions.
    1333              : #define ARMV8_SHA512 (1 << 6)
    1334              : 
    1335              : // ARMV8_SHA3 indicates support for eor3 instructions.
    1336              : #define ARMV8_SHA3 (1 << 7)

    1338              : #if defined(OPENSSL_STATIC_ARMCAP)
    1339              : // We assume `CRYPTO_is_*_capable` already checked static capabilities.
    1340              : inline uint32_t OPENSSL_get_armcap() { return 0; }
    1341              : #else
    1342              : // OPENSSL_armcap_P contains ARM CPU capabilities as a bitmask of the above
    1343              : // constants. This should only be accessed with `OPENSSL_get_armcap`.
    1344              : extern uint32_t OPENSSL_armcap_P;
    1345              : 
    1346              : // OPENSSL_get_armcap initializes the library if needed and returns ARM CPU
    1347              : // capabilities. It is marked as a const function so duplicate calls can be
    1348              : // merged by the compiler.
    1349              : OPENSSL_ATTR_CONST uint32_t OPENSSL_get_armcap();
    1350              : #endif  // OPENSSL_STATIC_ARMCAP
    1351              : 
    1352              : // Normalize some older feature flags to their modern ACLE values.
    1353              : // https://developer.arm.com/architectures/system-architectures/software-standards/acle
    1354              : #if defined(__ARM_NEON__) && !defined(__ARM_NEON)
    1355              : #define __ARM_NEON 1
    1356              : #endif
    1357              : #if defined(__ARM_FEATURE_CRYPTO)
    1358              : #if !defined(__ARM_FEATURE_AES)
    1359              : #define __ARM_FEATURE_AES 1
    1360              : #endif
    1361              : #if !defined(__ARM_FEATURE_SHA2)
    1362              : #define __ARM_FEATURE_SHA2 1
    1363              : #endif
    1364              : #endif
    1365              : 
    1366              : // CRYPTO_is_NEON_capable returns true if the current CPU has a NEON unit. If

    1368              : inline int CRYPTO_is_NEON_capable() {

    1370              :     !defined(OPENSSL_NO_STATIC_NEON_FOR_TESTING)
    1371              :   return 1;
    1372              : #else
    1373              :   return (OPENSSL_get_armcap() & ARMV7_NEON) != 0;
    1374              : #endif
    1375              : }
    1376              : 
    1377              : inline int CRYPTO_is_ARMv8_AES_capable() {

    1382              : #endif
    1383              : }
    1384              : 
    1385              : inline int CRYPTO_is_ARMv8_PMULL_capable() {

    1391              : }

    1393              : inline int CRYPTO_is_ARMv8_SHA1_capable() {
    1394              :   // SHA-1 and SHA-2 (only) share `__ARM_FEATURE_SHA2` but otherwise

    1402              : 
    1403              : inline int CRYPTO_is_ARMv8_SHA256_capable() {
    1404              :   // SHA-1 and SHA-2 (only) share `__ARM_FEATURE_SHA2` but otherwise
    1405              :   // are dealt with independently.
    1406              : #if defined(OPENSSL_STATIC_ARMCAP_SHA256) || defined(__ARM_FEATURE_SHA2)
    1407              :   return 1;
    1408              : #else
    1409              :   return (OPENSSL_get_armcap() & ARMV8_SHA256) != 0;
    1410              : #endif
    1411              : }
    1412              :

    1414              :   // There is no `OPENSSL_STATIC_ARMCAP_SHA512`.

    1419              : #endif

    1451              : // OPENSSL_vasprintf_internal is just like `vasprintf(3)`. If `system_malloc` is
    1452              : // 0, memory will be allocated with `OPENSSL_malloc` and must be freed with
    1453              : // `OPENSSL_free`. Otherwise the system `malloc` function is used and the memory
    1454              : // must be freed with the system `free` function.
    1455              : OPENSSL_EXPORT int OPENSSL_vasprintf_internal(char **str, const char *format,
    1456              :                                               va_list args, int system_malloc)
    1457              :     OPENSSL_PRINTF_FORMAT_FUNC(2, 0);

    1464              : // `CRYPTO_set_fuzzer_mode`. In non-fuzzer builds, this function statically
    1465              : // returns zero so the codepaths will be deleted by the optimizer.
    1466              : int CRYPTO_fuzzer_mode_enabled();
    1467              : #else
    1468            0 : inline int CRYPTO_fuzzer_mode_enabled() { return 0; }
    1469              : #endif

    1503            0 : }
    1504              : 
    1505              : inline uint64_t CRYPTO_addc_u64(uint64_t x, uint64_t y, uint64_t carry,

    1508            0 : }
    1509              : 
    1510              : #else
    1511              : 
    1512              : inline uint32_t CRYPTO_addc_u32(uint32_t x, uint32_t y, uint32_t carry,
    1513              :                                 uint32_t *out_carry) {
    1514              :   declassify_assert(carry <= 1);
    1515              : #if defined(_M_IX86)
    1516              :   uint32_t sum = 0;
    1517              :   *out_carry = _addcarry_u32(carry, x, y, &sum);

    1519              : #else
    1520              :   uint64_t ret = carry;
    1521              :   ret += (uint64_t)x + y;

    1523              :   return (uint32_t)ret;
    1524              : #endif
    1525              : }
    1526              : 
    1527              : inline uint64_t CRYPTO_addc_u64(uint64_t x, uint64_t y, uint64_t carry,

    1623              : 
    1624              : // Cleanup implements a custom scope guard, when the cleanup logic does not fit
    1625              : // in a destructor. Usage:
    1626              : //
    1627              : //     bssl::Cleanup cleanup = [&] { SomeCleanupWork(local_var); };
    1628              : template <typename F>
    1629              : class Cleanup {
    1630              :  public:
    1631              :   static_assert(std::is_invocable_v<F>);
    1632              :   static_assert(std::is_same_v<std::invoke_result_t<F>, void>);
    1633              : 
    1634              :   Cleanup(F func) : func_(func) {}
    1635              :   Cleanup(const Cleanup &) = delete;
    1636              :   Cleanup &operator=(const Cleanup &) = delete;
    1637              :   ~Cleanup() { func_(); }
    1638              : 
    1639              :  private:
    1640              :   F func_;
    1641              : };
    1642              : template <typename F>
    1643              : Cleanup(F func) -> Cleanup<F>;
    1644              : 
    1645              : // DECLARE_OPAQUE_STRUCT defines a public struct `public_name` with an
    1646              : // implementation struct `impl_name`.
    1647              : //
    1648              : // To prevent accidents, the `public_name` struct will be neither constructable,
    1649              : // nor copyable/movable, nor deletable.
    1650              : //
    1651              : // It must be used from inside the `bssl` namespace; however, `public_name` will
    1652              : // be defined outside.
    1653              : //
    1654              : // Usage:
    1655              : //
    1656              : // DECLARE_OPAQUE_STRUCT(public_st, PublicImpl)
    1657              : //
    1658              : // BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN
    1659              : //
    1660              : // class PublicImpl : public public_st {
    1661              : //  public:
    1662              : //   PublicImpl();
    1663              : //   ~PublicImpl();
    1664              : //   void foo();
    1665              : // };
    1666              : //
    1667              : // BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
    1668              : //
    1669              : // The implementation struct can be converted to the public struct implicitly;
    1670              : // to convert the public struct to the implementation struct, call
    1671              : // `FromOpaque` on it. It is explicitly allowed to call `FromOpaque` on a
    1672              : // `nullptr`.
    1673              : #define DECLARE_OPAQUE_STRUCT(public_name, impl_name)                  \
    1674              :   BSSL_NAMESPACE_BEGIN                                                 \
    1675              :   class impl_name;                                                     \
    1676              :   BSSL_NAMESPACE_END                                                   \
    1677              :                                                                        \
    1678              :   /* This is unnamespaced but assumed to not create linker symbols. */ \
    1679              :   struct public_name {                                                 \
    1680              :     using ImplType = bssl::impl_name;                                  \
    1681              :                                                                        \
    1682              :    private:                                                            \
    1683              :     public_name() = default;                                           \
    1684              :     ~public_name() = default;                                          \
    1685              :     public_name(const public_name &) = delete;                         \
    1686              :     public_name &operator=(const public_name &) = delete;              \
    1687              :                                                                        \
    1688              :     friend class bssl::impl_name;                                      \
    1689              :   };
    1690              : 
    1691              : template <typename Public>
    1692              : inline typename Public::ImplType *FromOpaque(Public *p) {
    1693              :   return static_cast<typename Public::ImplType *>(p);
    1694              : }
    1695              : 
    1696              : template <typename Public>
    1697              : inline const typename Public::ImplType *FromOpaque(const Public *p) {
    1698              :   return static_cast<const typename Public::ImplType *>(p);
    1699              : }
    1700              : 
    1701              : BSSL_NAMESPACE_END
    1702              : 
    1703              : 
    1704              : #endif  // OPENSSL_HEADER_CRYPTO_INTERNAL_H
